concepts.title: Young's Double Slit Experiment
concepts.conceptBody:
Young's Double Slit Experiment 

Thomas Young in the year 1801 first demonstrated the phenomenon of interference in light. He allowed sunlight to pass through a pinhole $\text{S}$ and then through two pinholes ${{\text{S}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$, placed at a good distance away from $\text{S}$ as shown in Fig. The interference pattern obtained on the screen $\text{AB}$ consists of a few colored bands of different widths with a white central band.

Now a days pinholes ${{S}_{1}}$ and ${{S}_{2}}$ are replaced by two narrow identical slits* (which increase the brightness) and sunlight by monochromatic light (which increases the number of bands). The pattern consists of a large number of equally spaced alternate bright and dark bands (called fringes) running parallel to the length of the-slit.

Through this experiment measuring the wavelength of light (characteristic of wave), Young conclusively established the wave-nature of light.

concepts.title: Principle of YDSE
concepts.conceptBody:
Principle of YDSE

Monochromatic light (single wavelength) from a narrow vertical slit S falls on two other narrow slits ${{\text{S}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ which are very close together and parallel to ${{\text{S}}_{\text{S}}}{{\text{S}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ act as two coherent sources (both. being derived from $\text{S}$ ). If $\text{S},{{\text{S}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ all are very narrow, diffraction (bending of light at openings whose width is of the order of wavelength of light) causes the emerging beams to spread into the region beyond the slits. Superposition occurs in the shaded area, where the diffracted beams overlap. Alternate bright and dark equally spaced vertical bands (interference fringes) can be observed on a screen placed at some distance from the slits. If either of ${{\text{S}}_{1}}$ or ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ is covered, the fringes disappear.

concepts.title: Working of YDSE
concepts.conceptBody:
Working of YDSE

An expression for the separation of two bright (or dark) fringes (called fringe width W) can be obtained as under.

Figure shows the light waves from ${{\text{S}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ meeting at an arbitrary point $\text{P}$ on the screen. Since $\text{D}>>\text{d}$, the two light rays are approximately parallel with a path difference. 
 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{ x} & ~={{\text{S}}_{2}}\text{P}-{{\text{S}}_{1}}\text{P}\approx \text{dsin}\theta  …(i) \end{array}$

For maximum intensity at $\text{P}$,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Delta\text{ x} =\text{n}\lambda & \left( \text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots \right) …(ii) \\ \text{ or }\text{ dsin}\theta & =\text{n}\lambda \left( \text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots \right) \end{array}$

The bright fringe corresponding to the integer $\text{n}$ is called the ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }}}$ order (or just ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }}}$) bright fringe. The bright fringe for $\text{n}=0$, is know as the central fringe and its center (point $\text{O}$) is called the central (or zero order) minimum. Higher order bright fringes are situated symmetrically about the central fringe.

For minimum intensity at $\text{P}$,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}  \Delta\text{ x}=\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\frac{\lambda }{2} & ~\left( \text{n}=\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots \right)…(iii) \\ \text{or d sin}\theta =\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\frac{\lambda }{2} & ~\left( \text{n}=\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots \right) \\ \end{array}$

The first minima $\left( \text{n}=\pm 1 \right)$ are adjacent to the central maximum on either side. It is useful to obtain expressions for the positions of the bright and dark fringes measured vertically from $\text{O}$. In addition to our assumption that $D>>d$, we assume that $d>>\lambda$. These can be valid assumptions because in practice $\text{D}$ is often of the order of $1 \text{ m}, \text{ d}$ a fraction of a millimeter and $\lambda $ a fraction of a micrometer for visible light. Under these conditions $\theta $ is small, thus, we can use the approximation,

$\text{sin}\theta \approx \text{tan}\theta =\frac{\text{y}}{\text{D}}$

Substituting ins Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get

\(\begin{array}{rr} \mathrm{y}_{\text {bright }}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}} & (\mathrm{n}=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots) \quad \ldots (iv) \\ \mathrm{y}_{\text {dark }}=\frac{(2 \mathrm{n}-1) \lambda \mathrm{D}}{2 \mathrm{~d}} & (\mathrm{n}= \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots) \quad \ldots (v) \end{array}\)

concepts.title: Fringe Width
concepts.conceptBody:
Fringe Width $\left( \beta \right)$ 

Distance between two adjacent bright (or dark) fringes is called the fringe width. It is denoted by $\beta $. Thus.

\(\begin{aligned} \beta &=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}-\frac{(n-1) \lambda D}{d} =\frac{\lambda D}{d} \\ \beta & =\frac{\lambda D}{d}\end{aligned}\)

Alternatively we can show that distance between two successive dark fringes is also $\frac{\lambda \text{D}}{\text{d}}$.

concepts.title: Angular Fringe Width
concepts.conceptBody:
Angular Fringe Width

As is very small, the equation $\text{tan}\theta =\frac{y}{D}$ can be reduced to

$\theta =y/D$ …(1)

Therefore $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta =\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }y}{D}$ …(2)

where $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }y$ gives the distance two consecutive similar fringes.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ y}=\beta $, the fringe width.

Hence from (1) we obtain.

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta =\frac{\beta }{\text{d}}$

But $\beta =\frac{\text{D}\lambda }{\text{d}}$, therefore from (2) we obtain.

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta =\frac{\lambda }{\text{d}}$

where $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta $ is called the angular fringe width.

concepts.title: Change in Fringe Width Due to Change in Medium
concepts.conceptBody:
Change in Fringe Width Due to Change in Medium  

If the light waves of wavelength $\lambda $ interfere, then the fringe width is given by

$\beta =\frac{\text{D}\lambda }{\text{d}}$

in a medium of refractive index $\mu $, the wavelength changes to

${\lambda }'=\frac{\lambda }{\mu }$ …(1)

The fringe width in the medium of refractive index $\mu $ is

${\beta }'=\frac{\text{D}{\lambda }'}{\text{d}}$ …(2)

From (1) and (2) we obtain.

$\Rightarrow   {\beta }'=\frac{\text{D}\lambda }{\mu \text{d}}$

This gives the new fringe width. From (1), differentiating we get,

$\begin{matrix}  \Delta \beta =\frac{\text{D}}{\text{d}} \Delta \lambda …(3) \\ \Rightarrow   \Delta \beta =\frac{\text{D}}{\text{d}}{{(\lambda -\lambda )}^{ ' }}  …(4) \\ \end{matrix}$

If the wavelength is constant, that is the medium is not changed, but slits separation is varied then,

$\Delta \beta =-\frac{\text{D}\lambda }{{\text{{d}'}}} \Delta\text{ {d}' }   \text{taking } 2 \text{ d}=\text{{d}'}$.

concepts.title: Intensity in YDSE
concepts.conceptBody:
Intensity in YDSE

For two coherent sources the resultant intensity is given by.

$\text{I}={{\text{I}}_{1}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}}\text{cos}\phi $

Putting ${{\text{I}}_{1}}={{\text{I}}_{2}}={{\text{I}}_{0}}$

We have $\text{I}={{\text{I}}_{0}}+{{\text{I}}_{0}}+2\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{I}}_{0}}}\text{cos}\phi $

Simplifying the above expression, we get

$\text{I}=4{{\text{I}}_{0}}\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\frac{\phi }{2}$

We can see that intensity is maximum at points where,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} & \text{ cos}\frac{\phi }{2}=\pm 1 & \text{ or }& \frac{\phi }{2}=n\pi \left( n=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots \right) \\ \text{ or } & \phi =2n\pi & \text{or } & \frac{2\pi }{\lambda }\Delta x=2n\pi \\ \text{ or } & \Delta x=n\lambda & \text{or } & d\text{sin}\theta =n\lambda \\ \text{ or } & \frac{\text{yd}}{\text{D}}=\text{n}\lambda & \text{or } & \text{y}=\frac{\text{nyD}}{\text{d}} \\ \end{array}$

and this condition, we have already discused earlier. Further, the maximum intensity is

${{\text{I}}_{\text{max }}}=4{{\text{I}}_{0}}$

Or $\text{I}={{\text{I}}_{\text{max}}}\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\frac{\phi }{2}$

Minimum intensity on the screen is found at points where,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ cos}\frac{\phi }{2}=0 & \text{ or } & \frac{\phi }{2}=\left( \text{n}-\frac{1}{2} \right)\pi \left( \text{n}=\pm 1,\pm 2,\pm 3\ldots \right) \\ or \phi =\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\pi & \text{ or } & \frac{2\pi }{\lambda }\Delta\text{ x}=\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\pi \\ or \Delta\text{ x}=\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\frac{\lambda }{2} & \text{ or } & \text{dsin}\theta =\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\frac{\lambda }{2} \\ or \frac{\text{yd}}{\text{D}}=\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\frac{\lambda }{2} & \text{ or } & \text{y}=\frac{\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\lambda \text{D}}{2} \\ \end{array}$

The minimum intensity is zero

concepts.title: Fringe Width with Wavelength
concepts.conceptBody:
Fringe Width with Wavelength

Fringe width $\left( \text{w} \right)$ is the distance between two successive maxima’s or minima’s. It is given by,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{w}=\frac{\lambda \text{D}}{\text{d}} \\ {} & \text{w}\propto \lambda \\ \end{array}$

Two conclusions can be drawn from this relation

1.If YDSE apparatus is immersed in a liquid of refractive index $\mu $, then wavelength of light and hence, fringe width decrease $\mu $ times.

2.If white light is used in place of a monochromatic light then coloured fringes are obtained on the screen with red fringes of larger size than that of violet, because ${{\lambda }_{\text{red }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}>{{\lambda }_{\text{violet }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$

But note that center is still white because path difference there is zero for all colors. Hence, all the wavelengths interfere constructively. At other places light will interfere destructively for those wavelengths for whom path difference is $\lambda /2,3\lambda /2,\ldots $ etc. and they will interfere constructively for the wavelengths for whom path difference is $\lambda ,2\lambda ,\ldots $ etc.

concepts.title: Shifting in Fringes
concepts.conceptBody:
Shifting in Fringes  

Suppose a glass slab of thickness $t$ and refractive index $\mu $ is inserted onto the path of the ray emanating from source ${{S}_{1}}$ then the whole fringe pattern shifts upwards by a distance $\frac{\left( \mu -\text{D} \right)\text{tD}}{\text{d}}$. This can be shown as under,

Geometric path difference between ${{\text{S}}_{2}}\text{P}$ and ${{\text{S}}_{1}}\text{P}$ is,

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}={{\text{S}}_{2}}\text{P}-{{\text{S}}_{1}}\text{P}=\frac{\text{yd}}{\text{D}}$

Path difference produced by the glass slab,

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{2}}=\left( \text{u}-1 \right)\text{t}$

Note : Due to the glass slab path of ray 1 gets increased by $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{2}}$.

Therefore, net path difference between the two rays is,

$\begin{matrix} \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}-\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{2}} \\  \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=\frac{\text{yd}}{\text{D}}-\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{t} \\ \end{matrix}$

For ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ maxima on upper side, $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=\text{n}\lambda $

Or $\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{\text{yd}}{\text{D}}-\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{t}=\text{n}\lambda \\ {} & \text{y}=\frac{\text{nyD}}{\text{d}}+\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{tD}}{\text{d}} \\ \end{array}$

Earlier it was $\frac{\text{nyD}}{\text{d}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Shift $=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{tD}}{\text{d}}$

Following three points are important with regard to above equation.

1.Shift is independent of $n$, (the order of the fringe), i.e.,

shift of zero order maximum $=$ shift of ${{7}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ order maximum

or shift of ${{5}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ order maximum $=$ shift of ${{9}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ order minimum and so on.

2.Shift is independent of $\lambda $, i.e., if white light is used then,

shift of red color fringe $=$ shift of violet color fringe.

3.Number of fringes shifted $=\frac{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ shift }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ fringe width }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}$

$=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)tD/d}{\lambda D/d}=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)t}{\lambda }$

These numbers are inversely proportional to $\lambda $. This is because shift is same for all colors but fringe width of the color having smaller value of $\lambda $ is small, so more number of fringes will shift of this color.
